steps.text: Contact the Office of the General Counsel to obtain specific information on treaties or legal analyses of treaties.
Determine whether any treaties exist.
Familiarize yourself with the treaty history, using resources such as the National Forest Service's interactive tribal connections map.
Consult other resources (such as the University of Oklahoma's Web site, University of Nebraska's Web site, Bureau of Indian Affairs, and Tribal Web sites) to obtain additional information.

preconditions.text: When beginning any outreach with Tribes

constraints.text: Federal Indian law generally preempts State laws that govern persons and property situated in a State.
The breadth and complexity of treaties with Native American Tribes is too great to present in this manual.

steps.text: Establish early communications and begin consultation as soon as practicable.
Consult in good faith throughout the agency’s decision making process and develop and maintain effective communication, coordination, and cooperation with Indian Tribes.
Coordinate with other Federal agencies and perform Tribal consultation jointly with those agencies, as appropriate and to the extent possible.
Establish mutually agreed protocols for open and timely communication describing proposed agency actions.
Establish agency points of contact.
Identify Tribal government leadership and staff level points of contact.
Develop mutually agreeable schedules for meetings between Tribal officials and representatives and the NRC’s management and staff.
Provide opportunities for Tribes to raise concerns on the effects that NRC licensing or regulatory activities may have on Tribal interests.
Seek to fully understand the concerns of the Tribe through consultation.

preconditions.text: The agency’s regulatory actions or proposed projects have Tribal implications.

constraints.text: Designated representatives of the NRC have the authority to speak for the agency in consultation with designated Tribal leaders, officials, and staff.
The NRC staff should be cognizant that Tribal governments may take a narrower interpretation of government to government consultation than the NRC staff does.

steps.text: Ensure that Tribal representatives understand the objectives of the consultation and know whether participants will be at the leadership or staff level.
Check with program offices and the Office of the General Counsel for specific guidance on complying with statutory provisions.

preconditions.text: When planning consultation activities with Tribes
When licensing actions and other agency regulatory activities require compliance with NHPA, NEPA, NWPA, and NAGPRA

steps.text: Plan for the time needed to consult to resolve adverse effects to historic properties at the beginning of the project planning process.
Meet with consulting parties to attempt to reach an agreement on how to resolve adverse effects.
Participate with consulting parties in the preparation of an agreement document (typically a Memorandum of Agreement [MOA] or Programmatic Agreement [PA]).
Request and consider ACHP comments.
Implement all the agreed upon measures established in the resulting MOA or PA.
Take steps to ensure that properties of religious importance (e.g., traditional cultural properties) are considered during the NHPA Section 106 review process.
Coordinate consultation with NHPA Section 106 consultation.

preconditions.text: The proposed undertaking poses a threat of adverse effects on historic properties
The signatories cannot come to an agreement

steps.text: Initiate dialogue through written correspondence announcing a regulatory action that may have Tribal implications.
Send copies of correspondence relating to NEPA or NHPA actions to the Tribal Historic Preservation Officer (THPO) or equivalent staff.
Conduct a follow-up meeting with Tribal officials or representatives.
Conduct subsequent interactions to contact and establish relationships with Tribal representatives who hold similar levels of authority.
Track and record communications by maintaining an administrative contact log.
Conduct a formal government to government meeting with a mutually approved agenda.
Consult with any Indian Tribe that attaches religious and cultural significance to historic properties that may be affected by the agency's undertaking.
Check with the program office to determine whether specific guidance exists for conducting the NHPA Section 106 process or evaluating historic and cultural resources.

preconditions.text: The actions relate to NEPA or the NHPA.
Expressions of interest are received from individual Tribes.

constraints.text: Correspondence should be between the leader of the Tribal government and an appropriate representative of NRC management.
Initial correspondence should describe the proposed regulatory action and identify the location and methods by which the Tribe may provide comments or request additional information.

steps.text: Exchange information and ideas with the appropriate Tribal attendees.
Allow sufficient time for Tribal representatives to caucus among themselves.
Solicit the views of Tribal attendees on the proposed NRC project or action.

preconditions.text: NRC staff or a license applicant, or both, make presentations

steps.text: Reach an agreement on processes or procedures for maintaining communication and resolving disputes.
Allow Tribal meeting participants the opportunity to review the NRC’s 'draft' summary to ensure an accurate record.
Give participants copies of the transcript and the opportunity to provide written corrections in the record.
Seek clarification of the issues and work to resolve the differences.
Summarize the topics discussed and any commitments made and identify outstanding issues.
Follow the general agency correspondence procedures for written correspondence sent to a Tribal representative.
Follow up with a telephone call to the Tribal contact.
Provide time for people to speak without interruption.
Contact the Office of Nuclear Material Safety and Safeguards (NMSS) Federal, State, and Tribal Liaison Branch for assistance in arranging for translators.
Address Tribal leaders by their official titles and names.
Introduce Tribal leaders before other participants and invite them to address the meeting first.
Display respect for the Tribe’s customs and beliefs if a meeting is opened with a prayer, song, or invocation.

preconditions.text: When parties dispute or disagree on the substance of a meeting summary
If a reply is not received within the expected time period
If Tribal members, especially Tribal elders, appreciate the option of having an interpreter present

constraints.text: Do not assume that commitments or agreements reached during a meeting will be final; Tribal representatives may need to obtain concurrence from Tribal leaders.

steps.text: Establish familiarity and continuity.
Keep the focus where it belongs by highlighting Tribes and their concerns during introductions and the meeting table.
Be adaptable by exhibiting flexibility, sensitivity, and empathy in Tribal relations.
Be patient and take time to understand, communicate with, and work with the Tribes who have an interest in NRC activities.
